The surgeon places a primary 12mm port, followed by an ancillary port in the right upper midline, and a subxiphoid port, ensuring the subxiphoid port is positioned above the liver.
The surgeon observes the liver, noting its relatively normal size, and confirms the absence of an obvious anterior hiatal hernia.
The surgeon visually inspects the anatomical structures, identifying the pylorus and the gallbladder, which is not being removed during this procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the pylorus and plans to leave approximately six centimeters of the antrum, marking a vertical line down from the lesser curvature as a reference point for the gastrectomy.
The surgeon decompresses the stomach, likely using a nasogastric tube or similar device, reducing its volume and preparing it for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon begins dissection slightly more than halfway down the greater curvature of the stomach using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the transverse colon mesentery is elevated during the process.
The surgeon continues dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach, maintaining proximity to the gastric wall.

Tony carefully grabs the cut edge of the stomach tissue to prevent a barbershop pole effect, ensuring precise alignment during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon adjusts the angulation of the stapler using wrist movement, ensuring it maintains a slight distance while approaching the bougie. The stapler is positioned to be equidistant from both anterior and posterior aspects of the stomach.

The surgeon wiggles the bougie, a 34 French, ensuring it is adequately positioned but not overly tight. The stapler is pulsed for approximately fifteen seconds before switching to blue cartridges. Tony continues to hold the cut edge securely.

The surgeon adjusts the angulation of the echelon-powered stapler in line with the anatomical crotch, ensuring the correct anterior and posterior distance. The stapler is positioned to head down towards the gastroesophageal junction, with the assistant providing a clear view under the area. The stapler is pulsed for fifteen seconds.
The assistant grabs the fundus, allowing the surgeon to adjust the angle of the stapler with their wrist. The fundus is positioned with ample freedom, and the surgeon prepares to move upwards.

The surgeon adjusts the angle of the stapler slightly off the staple line, ensuring it is positioned safely away from the hiatus. The bougie moves easily, indicating adequate space, and the tissue broadens slightly, avoiding a dumbbell shape.

The surgeon identifies the stretch located at the hiatus and post-hiatus area, where the cardia of the stomach is visible. The area appears to have enough space for fullness, and adjustments can be made if necessary.

The surgeon evaluates the sleeve gastrectomy, noting the room for adjustment and the importance of pulsing for hemostasis. The sleeve appears well-formed, and the tissue retracts quickly after manipulation, indicating effective stapling and hemostasis.

The surgeon examines the staple line, which appears smooth and intact without any kinks or twists. The assistant flips the tip of the staple line down, ensuring it is tapered for easier removal. The staple line maintains perfect integrity, with no wavering visible anteriorly or posteriorly.
The surgeon applies Evaseal to the staple line, aiming to enhance the hemostatic effect and ensure staple line integrity.
